Review article

Nanotechnology-enhanced fiber-reinforced polymer composites: Recent advancements on processing techniques and applications
==========================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Adib Bin Rashid a, Mahima Haque b, S M Mohaimenul Islam b, K.M. Rafi Uddin Labib b

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e24692](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e24692 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2405844024007230&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Abstract
--------

Incorporating [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can significantly improve the performance and functionality of fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites. Different techniques exist for processing, testing, and implementing nanocomposites in various industries. Depending on these factors, these materials can be tailored to suit the specific applications of the automotive and aerospace industries, defence industries, biomedical and energy sectors etc. Nanotechnology offers several potential benefits for composites, including improved mechanical properties, surface modification, and sensing capabilities. This paper discusses the different types of nanoparticles, [nanofibers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanofiber "Learn more about nanofibers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and nano-coating that can be used for reinforcement, surface modification, and property enhancement in FRP composites. It also examines the challenges associated with incorporating nanotechnology into composites and provides recommendations for potential opportunities in future work. This study is intended to offer a comprehensive understanding of the current research on using nanotechnology in FRP composites and its potential impact on the composites industry.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007205)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024001506)

Keywords
--------

Nanotechnology

Nanomaterial

Surface coating

Fiber reinforced

Polymer composites

Mechanical properties

1. Introduction
---------------

Composites are a category of [engineering materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/engineering-material "Learn more about engineering materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that have attracted much attention in various fields, including electronic equipment, sports, biomedical applications, aerospace, and automotive applications. They are composed of two or more elements, each with distinct traits such as high stiffness, [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), lower thermal expansion, and resistance to environmental degradation. The need for composites arises because it is often seen that a single element does not always possess all desired properties. However, this can be achieved by selectively combining different materials to achieve the desired outcome [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib1)]. The fundamental components of composites are the matrix and the reinforcement. Depending on the matrix and reinforcement chosen, the composite type also varies. Composites can be classified as ceramic matrix, metal matrix, [polymer matrix](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-matrix "Learn more about polymer matrix from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), particle-reinforced, fiber-reinforced, and nanocomposite. Reinforcements act as load-bearing components with their strength and [rigidity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/rigidity "Learn more about rigidity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), whereas matrix materials are like binders that hold the reinforcements together [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib2)].

Fiber-reinforced composites, which contain fibers as the reinforcement held together by a matrix, are commonly utilized because they possess exceptional [mechanical characteristics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-property "Learn more about mechanical characteristics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), can be customized to specific requirements, exhibit excellent endurance against fatigue and corrosion, and have functional stiffness and strength due to the presence of fibers. The fibers can be synthetic fibers such as carbon fiber, glass fiber, or [aramid fibers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aramid-fiber "Learn more about aramid fibers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [natural fibers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/natural-fibre "Learn more about natural fibers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as animal fiber, plant fiber, or mineral fiber. Owing to their unique properties, fiber-reinforced [polymer composites](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-composite "Learn more about polymer composites from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (FRPCs) are used in many sectors. Some examples of the applications of fiber-reinforced composites investigated by researchers include manufacturing turbine blades with silicon carbide-ceramic matrix composites reinforced by carbon fibers [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib3)]. Polymer-based actuators were used as flexible links for robotics-based assemblies [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib4)]. It has been seen that adding high-strength fibers can improve the properties of materials. When used for gear pairs, 28% of glass fiber content in polyoxymethylene (POM) provided a substantial improvement of 50% (approximately) in load-bearing capacity and a reduced specific rate of wear when compared to an unreinforced polyoxymethylene POM [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib5)]. Fiber-reinforced polymers are also suitable for structural applications in the aerospace, marine, and automotive sectors. Structural designs were performed on an automobile hood utilizing a flax/vinyl ester composite and compared to a metal hood structure. Commercial [Finite Element Method](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/finite-element-method "Learn more about Finite Element Method from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (FEM) software confirmed the natural composite design's structural stability, weight, and safety. Structural testing also corroborated the acceptability hood structure regarding stability and safety [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib6)].

Using glass fiber and carbon fiber hybrid composites could enhance the mechanical properties of the polymer composite, making it suitable for marine. In a study comparing plain glass fiber reinforced polymer composites and hybrid composites, seawater-aged plain carbon ([C]s) and plain glass ([G]s) woven [GCG2C]S type hybrid composite showed improved flexural, [tensile strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/ultimate-tensile-strength "Learn more about tensile strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and modulus, but reduced [impact strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/impact-strength "Learn more about impact strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib7)]. The exceptional material qualities of carbon fiber reinforced composites, such as their resistance to corrosion, impressive strength-to-weight ratio, temperature changes and fatigue, and low maintenance costs, make them an ideal option for [propellers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/propeller "Learn more about propellers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") used in marine applications [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib8)]. Sandwich composite panels with glass or carbon fiber skins and a polymeric core have been implemented to develop marine craft structures and complete hulls [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib9)]. Using natural and synthetic fibers in hybrid composites to promote environmentally friendly production has been investigated by many researchers. Composites made from ramie fiber provided a 12–14% reduction in weight when used in aircraft wing boxes [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib10)]. Hybrid composites reinforced with glass fiber and [kenaf fiber](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/kenaf-fiber "Learn more about kenaf fiber from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") were suitable for use as aircraft radome due to their ability to resist rain erosion and favorable mechanical properties. Green composites made from [bast fibers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/bast-fibre "Learn more about bast fibers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") were suitable for non-load-carrying structures like seat cushions, parcel shelves, and cabin linings. The primary concern for airframe structure applications is ensuring compliance with fire, smoke, and toxicity standards (FST). To enhance the fire retardancy of natural fiber, applying [fire retardants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/flame-retardant "Learn more about fire retardants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to its surface was necessary since its fire resistance is lower than synthetic fibers. Honeycomb core composites with woven flax fabrics were ideal for interior panels of aircraft cabins [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib11)]. Composites containing carbon and cellulose-based [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") offer benefits like compactness, lightness, and temperature resistance, but there's limited research on their potential for mechanical uses.

[Polymer nanocomposites](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-nanocomposites "Learn more about Polymer nanocomposites from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are employed in diverse areas, from biotechnology and nano-electronics to super-capacitors and biosensors. They are also integrated into mechanical applications such as solar cells and radar materials. The interaction between nanoparticles and polymers depends on the polymer type, affecting their properties [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib12)]. Nanotechnology enhances biosensors with machine learning to measure trace substances precisely and differentiate complex signals. State-of-the-art machine learning models, including CNNs and RNNs, are increasingly utilized in sensor data analysis, replacing traditional regression analysis. Nanocomposites are vital in healthcare, supporting disease prevention, personalized medicine, and diagnostics.

Additionally, they improve liquid crystal biosensors, enhancing sensitivity and detection capabilities. These sensors are cost-efficient, straightforward to manufacture and deliver reliable results [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib13)]. Carbon [nanomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanocrystalline-material "Learn more about nanomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CNMs) derived from biological sources, such as plants, are widely used, especially in energy storage technologies like batteries, super-capacitors, and fuel cells, replacing synthetic graphite and activated carbon. Adopting these materials can reshape energy storage technologies, contributing to a more sustainable future and circular economy [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib14)]. Rashid et al. proposed a hand-loomed [jute fiber](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/jute-fiber "Learn more about jute fiber from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") mat method and its use in fabricating epoxy jute fiber composites. Different fiber arrangements and materials were explored to enhance mechanical properties. The study suggested a simple technique for producing hybrid composites with improved mechanical properties [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib15)].

In conclusion, composites are materials that combine distinct components to achieve desired properties, making them widely applicable in various industries. Fabricating fiber-reinforced and hybrid composites has led to exceptional mechanical properties and increased sustainability. Ongoing research in composite materials continues to explore new possibilities for customized material properties and further advancements.

However, the properties of fiber-reinforced polymer composites can also be further enhanced. Adding fillers to polymer matrices presents another approach to augment mechanical properties and reduce material costs. Khan et al. reviewed the effects of adding [aerogels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aerogels "Learn more about aerogels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") into composites. The aerospace sector commonly employs inorganic [oxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxide-compound "Learn more about oxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") aerogels and composites due to their remarkable capacity to endure elevated temperatures, low [thermal conductivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/thermal-conductivity "Learn more about thermal conductivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), ease of shaping, and processability. This classification primarily includes single-component oxide aerogels and composites like SiO 2, Al 2 O 3, and ZrO 2, as well as multi-component oxide aerogels and composites such as SiO 2–Al 2 O 2 and SiO 2–ZrO 2 [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib16)]. Gelatin-based biomaterials for various applications were studied by Rashid et al. [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib17)]. Recently, much focus has been directed toward adding nano-sized fillers into composites. These materials are called nanocomposites. They are a different class of composites known for their superior properties to regular composite materials. They can be manufactured by distributing nanoparticles in the matrix or coating the nanofillers onto the fiber before making the composite. Because of their large surface area, nanoparticles provide a stronger bonding between the fiber and matrix, facilitating effective stress transfer. In addition, including fillers can decrease the number of unoccupied spaces and improve the rigidity of the laminated material. This is because the filler material facilitates better interaction between the fiber and the matrix, which allows for a smoother transfer of stress from the polymer matrix to the reinforced fiber when subjected to external forces. As a result, the mechanical characteristics of the material are improved [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib18)]. Rashid et al. studied the application of polymer nanocomposites in the defense sector. PNCs are highly versatile materials used in the defense industry for manufacturing ballistic protection systems, aircraft components, sensors, communication systems, and energy storage. They offer several advantages over traditional materials and are expected to continue to expand in their use, making them an essential component of modern warfare [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib19)]. Haque et al. studied the impact of incorporating different nanofillers in glass fiber and jute fiber hybrid composites. It was found that adding MWCNT as a secondary reinforcement significantly enhanced the tensile, flexural, flame retardancy and water absorption properties [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib20)]. Rashid et al. explored the utilization of [cellulose nanocrystals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/cellulose-nanocrystals "Learn more about cellulose nanocrystals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CNCs) and functionalized nanocrystals to enhance the performance of nano-fibrillated cellulose composites through methods like physical adsorption, surface grating, and [chemical vapor deposition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/chemical-vapor-deposition "Learn more about chemical vapor deposition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). CNCs possess exceptional chemical, physical and biological properties, making them advanced biomaterials.

As a result, the composites are under extensive research for potential applications in engineering and medical industries [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib21)]. Several reports have suggested that adding small nanofillers to a polymer matrix, such as nano clay, [silica nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/silica-nanoparticle "Learn more about silica nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and carbon nanotubes, can enhance composites' reliability and properties. This includes improvements in tensile strength, [tensile modulus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/youngs-modulus "Learn more about tensile modulus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), impact strength, and elongation at break [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib22)]. The application of [nanocomposite coatings](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanocomposite-coating "Learn more about nanocomposite coatings from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on turbine blades of jet engines was found to inhibit grain growth at high temperatures, making them suitable for use as overlay coatings in multiple aerospace applications. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#fig1) shows the benefits of adding nano-sized reinforcements into composites compared to the conventional unmodified composites.

![Image 11: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2405844024007230-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (621KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2405844024007230-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (621KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2405844024007230-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Benefits of nanocomposites over traditional composites.

Robust nanocomposite coatings were explicitly developed for aerospace applications [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib23)]. [Carbon nanofibers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-nanofiber "Learn more about Carbon nanofibers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are increasingly used to improve composites' mechanical and electrical properties. However, for these nanofillers to be effective, they need to be evenly dispersed throughout the polymer matrix. Rhodes et al. fabricated a composite material of hyperbranched [polyol](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polyol "Learn more about polyol from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and carbon [nanofibers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanofiber "Learn more about nanofibers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to address this issue, improving dispersibility within the [polymer network](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-network "Learn more about polymer network from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib24)]. The impact of different processing parameters on the morphology and electrical properties of [nanocomposite films](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanocomposite-film "Learn more about nanocomposite films from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was examined by Laurenzi et al., explicitly focusing on the effect of nozzle diameter, the distance between nozzle and target substrate, and the concentration of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib25)]. When nanocomposite coatings were applied to flexible membranes, several valuable properties relevant to aerospace applications, including thermal blankets and charging mitigation layers, were obtained.

Li et al. investigated the characteristics of composites made of epoxy/hyperbranched polymers with amino-terminal groups and glass fibers. They found that these composites exhibited significant improvements in impact resistance, elongation at break, and [tensile properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/tensile-property "Learn more about tensile properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") compared to composites made with unmodified epoxy/polyamide [thermosets](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/thermoset-plastics "Learn more about thermosets from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib26)]. Rashid et al. studied the different methods of 3D-printing biomaterials, which is a popular technique for manufacturing composites [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib27)]. Tzounis et al. successfully developed high-performance composites with hierarchical reinforcement using [natural rubber](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/rubber "Learn more about natural rubber from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), short jute fibers (JF), and carbon nanotubes (CNTs). Surface modification of the jute fiber (JF) with CNT networks produced a hierarchical structure that significantly improved the composite materials' mechanical properties. This hierarchical structure enhanced the wetting property of the natural rubber and improved mechanical interlocking at the interface [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024007230#bib28)]. Rashid et al. locally made a creep testing machine to study high-temperature material properties. Tests were conducted on an [aluminium alloy](https://www

enriched_points.expanded_content: Functional nanocomposites offer enhanced mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties, making them superior to traditional materials across various applications, including structural and biomedical fields.
These materials demonstrate versatile applicability in sectors like aerospace, automotive, and biomedical, facilitating innovation through shared technologies and benefits across industries.
Nanocomposites can be engineered to serve multiple purposes, such as energy storage, structural reinforcement, and environmental applications, thus broadening their utility in modern engineering.
Despite their advantages, challenges include processing complexities, cost implications, and potential environmental impacts that require careful consideration and ongoing research.

The future of functional nanocomposites is promising, with advancements in nanotechnology expected to unlock new applications, improve sustainability, and enhance performance across diverse industries.
